title: The role of the insula in interoception and trauma awareness
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
You have a body. You just might not feel it. 

 I spent years living in my head. Completely. My body was just a vehicle for my brain, a thing I dragged ar...
htmlContent:
You have a body. You just might not feel it.

I spent years living in my head. Completely. My body was just a vehicle for my brain, a thing I dragged around while I thought, planned, worried, and dissociated. I could feel panic in my chest, sure. But a subtle hunger cue? The early warning signs of exhaustion? The quiet hum of safety in my muscles? Nothing. I was a ghost piloting a meat suit, and I had no idea that this disconnection was actually a core symptom of my trauma.

Here's the thing. There is a small, folded region of your brain called the insula. It's buried deep in the cerebral cortex, tucked between the temporal and frontal lobes. And it is the organ that reads your body's internal state. Your heartbeat. Your breath. The stretch of your stomach. The temperature of your skin. The insula is the bridge between your physical body and your conscious awareness of being alive. Without it working well, you don't just feel numb. You lose access to a fundamental stream of data that tells you who you are, what you need, and whether you are safe.

This is not abstract neuroscience. This is the story of why you might not know you're hungry until you're shaking. Why you can't tell you're sad until you're sobbing. Why you feel like a stranger in your own skin. Stay with me here. We're going to look at the insula, interoception, and how trauma literally rewires your ability to feel yourself from the inside out.

What is interoception, really?

Interoception is the sense of the internal state of your body. It's not touch (that's exteroception). It's not balance (that's proprioception). It's the perception of your heartbeat, your breathing, your digestion, your bladder fullness, your muscle tension, your inflammation. It's the feeling of your own aliveness.

Dr. Sarah Garfinkel, a cognitive neuroscientist at University College London, has done incredible work on this. She studies how accurately people can detect their own heartbeat. It sounds simple. You sit quietly. You count your heartbeats without taking your pulse. Then you compare that count to an actual ECG reading. Most people are not great at it. But people with anxiety disorders? They tend to be hyperaccurate. Their insula is screaming at them. People with depression, PTSD, or chronic dissociation? They tend to be way off. Their insula has gone quiet.

Think about it. Your brain is receiving signals from your body all the time. Every second. Your heart pumps. Your lungs expand. Your gut churns. These signals travel up the vagus nerve and into the brainstem, then up into the insula. The insula integrates them into a coherent map of "how I feel right now." This map is what gives you a sense of being embodied, grounded, and present.

But trauma changes this. It can make the map too loud, so you feel every flutter and twitch as a catastrophe. Or it can make the map too quiet, so you drift through life disconnected, numb, and confused about what you actually feel. Both are survival adaptations. Neither feels good.

How trauma silences the insula

I want you to imagine a smoke alarm. A good smoke alarm detects a small amount of smoke and makes a moderate noise. A traumatized smoke alarm either goes off at the faintest whiff of toast or stays completely silent while the kitchen is on fire. The insula is the same.

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote a book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), talks about this extensively. His research at Boston University showed that people with PTSD have significantly reduced insula activation when asked to focus on their bodily sensations. Their brains have learned that paying attention to the body is dangerous. The body holds unbearable memories. It holds pain. It holds terror. So the brain does what any good survival system does: it turns down the volume.

This is not a choice. It is not a failure. It is a protective strategy. But the cost is enormous. When you cannot feel your body, you cannot feel your emotions. Emotions are, at their core, physical sensations that your brain then interprets. Fear is a racing heart, shallow breath, and cold hands. Sadness is a heavy chest, a lump in the throat, and watery eyes. Joy is a lightness in the ribs and a warmth spreading through your torso. Cut off the body, and you cut off the entire emotional spectrum. You are left with thoughts about feelings, not feelings themselves.

And here is the cruel irony. The insula is also responsible for empathy. For feeling what others feel. For the resonance that makes relationships meaningful. When your insula goes quiet, you don't just lose yourself. You lose connection to other people. You become isolated inside your own head, watching life happen from behind a pane of glass.

Rebuilding interoceptive awareness

So what do you do when your insula has been trained to ignore your body? The answer is slow, careful, and sometimes boring. You retrain the insula to listen again. But you have to do it in a way that does not retraumatize you.

Dr. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about this in terms of neuroception. Your nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety or danger. If you force yourself to feel your body before your system is ready, you can trigger a flood of overwhelm. The key is to start with cues of safety. Warmth. A soft blanket. A familiar voice. Gentle movement. Only then do you invite the insula to slowly, tentatively, come back online.

One practice that helps is the "gliding breath." You sit or lie down. You place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. You breathe in for four counts, hold for four, and breathe out for six. You are not trying to feel anything. You are just giving your insula a gentle, predictable signal. Over time, the insula starts to trust that paying attention to the body is not going to cause a catastrophe. It begins to send clearer signals. You might notice a subtle vibration in your chest. A slight coolness in your nose. A tiny flutter in your stomach. These are not problems to fix. They are data. They are your insula whispering, "I am here."

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link), which explores how family histories of trauma can shape your nervous system before you are even born. Understanding the source of the silence can make it easier to be patient with the process of coming back.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It is an amino acid found in green tea that can help calm the hypervigilant insula without sedation. It is not a cure. But it can create a window of safety where the body feels less threatening, making it easier to practice interoceptive awareness.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Dr. Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It shifts the question from "What is wrong with you?" to "What happened to you?" and that shift alone can change how your insula relates to your history.

The insula is not broken. It is protecting you.

I need you to hear this. Your insula is not damaged. It is not defective. It has been shaped by experience to do exactly what it does. If you dissociate, your insula learned that feeling your body was dangerous. If you hyperventilate at every heartbeat, your insula learned that paying close attention was the only way to survive. These are not failures. They are brilliant adaptations to impossible circumstances.

But adaptations can be updated. The insula has neuroplasticity. It can learn new patterns. It can soften. It can expand. It can learn to feel a gentle heartbeat and interpret it as safety, not threat. It can learn to feel hunger as a friendly signal, not an emergency. It can learn to feel sadness as a wave that passes, not a drowning.

This takes time. It takes patience. It takes a kind of gentleness that trauma survivors are not usually taught to have for themselves. You might feel nothing for weeks. Then a flicker. Then a flood. Then nothing again. That is normal. That is the insula recalibrating. Wild, right? A tiny piece of your brain is doing the slow, patient work of learning to trust your own body again.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you tighten anywhere? Did you hold your breath? Did you drift away from reading this paragraph? That is your insula responding to the content. That is data. And that is a beginning.

Sit with that.

You are not broken. You are learning to feel. And feeling, even when it is uncomfortable, is the path back to being alive.
